title: The bride wore Anthro — BEAU SOCIETY issue #081
author: beausociety
raw_article:
Hi guys!!

We’re getting into the swing of wedding season, huh?? A few weekends ago, we went to a wedding for our close friends, Sam Darnold and Katie Hoofnagle. This was our first time attending a wedding as husband and wife (and mom and dad!), and everything hit differently. The love in the air felt palpable and reminiscent. And I actually don’t think there’s anything more fun than being at a wedding with your husband! 10/10 recommend.

So many small details about Sam and Katie’s wedding felt like them. Katie never went wedding-dress shopping — she found her dress at an Anthropologie in Orange County, where she worked in college! She wore her everyday jewelry, but added a few sentimental pieces, like a watch, a gift from her now-husband, and a special ‘something blue.’ Her photographer was a friend from preschool, and there were no phones allowed at the wedding…I mean, anyone who knows me knows I’m a fan of any event where that’s a rule.

As I was reflecting on the wedding this week, I texted Katie a few questions about her planning process and bridal POV. She’s the coolest!

HAILEE: Shall we start with how you and Sam met??

KATIE: We were introduced through a mutual friend at a bar in Charlotte, NC, where we were both living and working at the time.

HAILEE: How long were you together before you got engaged? Were you surprised?

KATIE: We dated for about 2 years prior to getting engaged. I knew it was coming, but Sam caught me off guard the day he actually proposed.

HAILEE: When you and Sam were starting to plan your wedding, what was most important to you?

KATIE: Sam and I have always said how fortunate we are to be surrounded by the most incredible friends and family. When we made our guest list, it was about 235 people, and every single person on that list was an important individual.

We wanted to have a Friday wedding so that we could spend the entire weekend with our guests. Our wedding was in San Diego, which was a ‘destination wedding’ for the vast majority of our friends. We wanted to give everyone the opportunity to make a trip out of it and spend Saturday together decompressing by the pool.

HAILEE: There’s a lot of general fuss around weddings. What was the most absurd thing you came across as you were planning?

KATIE: The amount of options for everything…the illusion of choice is so real. Sam and I had to make a conscious effort to be decisive when making decisions rather than dwelling on the details.

HAILEE: I loved your dress! You found it at Anthropologie?? And you worked there in college? What a cool full-circle moment!

KATIE: Yes!! I worked at Anthropologie in college and was always drawn to their wedding line [BHLDN]. I love their sophisticated yet artistic brand voice that combines a romantic, boho feel with a modern twist. About a week after I got engaged, I happened to walk by their storefront in Orange County and saw my dress hanging in the window. I fell in love with it and bought it after trying it on. I never went to another bridal store.

HAILEE: So you bought it off the rack, and it worked perfectly?

KATIE: It did! Anthropologie wedding dresses are sized in the standard women’s sizing format, so I purchased my normal size and had very minimal alterations. This was an ideal situation given our short window between engagement and wedding, as well as being in Seattle for the majority of the wedding planning process.

HAILEE: What was one thing you did that made you feel like YOU on your wedding day?

KATIE: I wore my everyday jewelry! I added a few special pieces, but I felt like myself with my normal earrings, rings, and bracelets on. Most of the jewelry I wear every day was a gift from either my parents or grandparents. One of my favorite pieces is a birthstone from my grandparents that I recently had reset into a pinky ring as my ‘something blue.’

HAILEE: You had so many personal touches woven throughout the day. Even your photographer was a childhood friend, right?

KATIE: Yes! Stephanie and I met in preschool and attended the same high school. She’s an incredibly talented photographer, and after watching her business grow and thrive over the last few years, we knew we wanted her to shoot our wedding.

HAILEE: Did you have a bridal party?

KATIE: I didn’t have a bridal party, but I invited my mom, grandmother, sister, and cousin, along with Sam’s mom, sister, and her fianceé to get ready with me.

HAILEE: What was the most unexpectedly beautiful part of the day for you? A small moment you loved but maybe didn’t anticipate being so special.

KATIE: Sam and I did our vows twice. Once over coffee before the day started, and again at our ceremony in front of everybody. I felt so present and at peace standing up there during the ceremony, having already had the opportunity to say mine and hear Sam’s.

HAILEE: Talk about that walk down the aisle for you.

KATIE: We had a beautiful string quartet perform before, during, and after the ceremony. Sam walked down the aisle to “Georgia on My Mind” by Ray Charles, a nod to The Masters since the tournament holds special meaning to both of us. I walked down to “Forever” by Noah Kahan, since he’s one of the artists Sam and I have seen together live in concert.

HAILEE: I loved your father-daughter dance at the reception! Tell me about that moment.

KATIE: My dad and I have a very goofy and easy-going relationship, and we wanted everyone in the room to feel that while we danced. We went with the upbeat song “Crazy Little Thing Called Love” by Queen, and then invited everyone back onto the dance floor.

HAILEE: A question from Josh: What was something you and Sam HAD to have at your wedding? (For reference, his number-one thing was a big black-and-white checkered dance floor.)

KATIE: In-N-Out and espresso martinis at the after party!

HAILEE: I love that you decided to have your wedding on a Friday so Saturday could be a chill hangout day! What was the day-after situation?

KATIE: Following our Saturday brunch, Sam reserved a huge cabana by the pool with an open invite for friends to come hang out. We ended up having a group of about 50 friends who stuck around and spent the afternoon sharing stories from the night before.

HAILEE: Now that you’re on the other side, what are your DOs and DON’Ts of wedding planning?

KATIE:

DOs:

  • If you’re able to, hire a planner! Having someone knowledgeable about the wedding industry and well-connected with vendors makes all the difference.

  • Have the bulk of your speeches at the welcome party and save wedding-day speeches for parents only. This not only saves time at the reception, but it also allows parents to have a special moment.

  • Reserve the first few rows of your ceremony seating for family. We had a seating chart so everyone knew exactly where they were going.

  • Take a moment immediately after the ceremony concludes to be with your partner. Our planner and photographer directed us to a private area before extended family photos started so we could have a moment to ourselves.

DONTs:

  • Don’t dwell on the small details. You won’t remember the little things when it’s all said and done.

  • Don’t feel like you need to do everything on your own; it’s okay to ask for help!

  • Don’t spend too much time on the invitations. They’re beautifully displayed on people’s refrigerators for a few months, and then they’re recycled.

  • Don’t procrastinate! Planning a wedding can be daunting, but the sooner you start making decisions, the better.

BS WITH KATIE HOOFNAGLE

H: Every wedding should have…
K: A live band.

H: No wedding should have…
K: Phones(!!!) highly recommend finding a way to do a phone-free wedding day, so everyone is present for the entirety of the day and night.

H: A wedding that inspired you (maybe one you saw in a magazine or went to when you were growing up)...
K: Yours :) The importance of understated elegance was a key point throughout our planning process.

H: That’s so sweet! Thank you! Your wedding-day beauty/wellness essentials were...
K: Electrolytes, undereye patches, and a good moisturizer first thing in the morning for skin prep.

H: The best thing you ate at your wedding was…
K: Double Double from In-N-Out while I changed into my second-look dress.

H: The best song of the night was…
K: “Mr. Brightside” (performed by the band).

H: Looking back two weeks later, the best moment was…
K: Looking around the room on our wedding day, seeing friends from preschool, college, and football who didn’t know each other, laughing and talking and smiling like they’d been friends forever — it was such a surreal moment for us.

H: Your wedding day BS…
K: Big smiles!!! :))

COSIGN THAT ONE! Now for some fun wedding extras…

TO WEAR TO A WEDDING: Literally anything pulled from our new BS wedding guest edit!

FOR THE BRIDES: If I were doing it again, these pieces would be on my list.

FOR ‘SOMETHING BLUE’: These dangly (clip-on!) earrings, a perfect Mother-of-the-bride dress, and so much more.

TO HAVE & TO HOLD: ‘Til death do us part.

FOR THE BACHELORETTE: Or honeymoon! Or day-after-wedding pool day! Linen, crochet, and my favorite flip-flops. Great finds for summer, if you’re into whites and creams.

TO TAKE WITH YOU: “Before you marry a person, you should first make them use a computer with slow internet to see who they really are.” —Will Ferrell

Love,
Hailee
(Beau)

Highlighting your comments on the previous week’s issue. Today, Katie’s response to It all started on MTV…

“Thanks for the recommendation to check out the Extremely Helpful newsletter - I loved it and am binging the Wisecrack podcast this weekend as a result. Love that this newsletter is lifting up and elevating other voices as well.” -Katie (MBS)

title: It all started on MTV... — BEAU SOCIETY issue #080
author: beausociety
raw_article:
I love fashion. I’ve always been intrigued and fascinated by it. Fashion touches nearly every aspect of my life: from the work I do as an actor, to events and appearances related to my work, to my ever-evolving personal style, which I’m especially excited to hone in this new stage of my life. My fashion curiosity has grown exponentially through my relationship with Rob Zangardi and Mariel Haenn, my two amazing stylists, who have taught me so much about style and brought me into their creative process in an incredibly collaborative way.

The three of us have been friends for over a decade. R&M (that’s what I call them) have dressed me for music videos, MET Galas, Oscars, my wedding day, and every event in between. We’ll dive deeper into the stories of some of those specific fittings next week in MBS. But this week, I wanted R&M to come on to tell you about their careers because while they’ve taught me everything I know about fashion, they’ve also been instrumental in modeling how to work hard, trust yourself and others, form lasting relationships, and have a lot of fun along the way to building a dream life.

In our conversation, we get into how these two first got their foot in the door of the fashion industry (TRL anyone?), what a day in the life of a stylist really looks like, plus we relive our favorite looks from the past ten years — some real throwbacks!

Now onto my stylists and close friends: Rob and Mariel!

So many of our readers love fashion, but it seems like an intimidating industry to break into. How did you two first get into styling?

R: I kind of fell into it. My twin brother Joey had moved to New York and was working in casting at MTV. He introduced me to the wardrobe department, and they took a chance on me. I was a kid from Ohio, suddenly in Times Square watching the ball drop on New Year’s Eve, surrounded by celebrities. It was one of those real “how did I get here?” moments. That was also the height of MTV and TRL. It was the most incredible time. A dream I could have never imagined, and honestly, the thing that set up the rest of my life. I owe everything to MTV and my brother.

M: I was in high school and was always drawn to fashion. It was the ‘90s. Jennifer Lopez and Gwen Stefani videos were dominating the charts on the MTV Top Video Countdown. I would watch their videos and think, ‘I don’t know what it’s called, but I want to dress pop stars for their music videos!’ I was 15 years old.

What was it like in those early days working at MTV?

R: MTV back then had all the access in the world. Every celebrity came through. Red carpets, award shows, appearances, we had access to everything. It was also the very beginning of the Internet. We had actual Rolodexes filled with business cards. If we got a new VJ or client, like Mandy Moore, we would messenger over their comp cards to brands like Chanel to get them interested in dressing them. We were also working off physical lookbooks, not Vogue.com. You’d sit there flipping pages and making selects. Everything was somehow slower, but at the same time, we were still dealing with the same last-minute chaos we live in today. And if there was a big red carpet, you were hoping your work would end up in print a month later or in the newspaper the next day. The back of the tabloids was both our best friend and worst enemy. You could be best-dressed in Vogue and worst-dressed in Us Weekly for the exact same outfit. It was fun. It was chaotic. And it was everything.

M: Working at MTV in the late ‘90s was such a vibe. We had so many different types of celebrities come through those hallways. In a way, it kinda felt like high school all over again. Standing in the hallways after getting my VJs dressed, waiting for the live guest of the day…it was exhilarating to say the least. It was also so much fun since I had already worked in the music industry prior to my job at MTV. It was like a reunion when I saw artists and managers I already had relationships with. It definitely felt like I was growing up and evolving from where I started. One of my greatest memories was waiting for the weeklies to come out (US Weekly, InStyle, etc.) to see how my work was judged, so to speak. I loved seeing any of the looks I styled printed in the magazines.

When did you two meet, and what made you realize early on that you worked well as a team?

R: We met at MTV a few years later and immediately became best friends. Not long after, we became partners. Our first job together was the Rihanna “Umbrella” video, which is kind of insane when you think about it. What an epic way to start a partnership. We came from completely different worlds, but somehow had the exact same eye. We would walk into a showroom and pull the exact same thing without even talking. It just always worked. I’m thankful for that partnership every day.

M: Rob worked in the same wardrobe department as me, but he was there before I started. He came back to visit; he was friends with Suchin Pak (one of my VJs). We instantly connected. He said he needed to pull jewelry for a shoot he was doing. I said, “Why don’t you look at what I have…take whatever you need!” which in hindsight was very trusting, as we had just met!

Okay, I have to know, what was it like styling Rihanna!?

R: It was wild — green screen, rain, fire, Jay-Z, dancers, puddles everywhere. It definitely tested us. But it’s still one of the most memorable jobs we’ve ever done.

M: Oh man…too many memories to count. All I can say is that there was a time when we only slept on airplanes. She was my first client and my first everything. I’ll always hold a special place in my heart for her! We grew up together and made such a statement with our work together.

Do you remember meeting me for the first time? What were your first impressions? (Be honest)!

R: It feels like we’ve worked together forever. It feels like family…and I still can’t believe you’ve added to your family. Mariel and I are so excited for you and Josh, and we cannot wait to dress this baby.

M: Oh gosh, I’ve literally been racking my brain. Honestly, I don’t remember the first time, but I have a very clear memory of when I rescued my dog Nigel and brought him to the office for a fitting. He was so small and cold that I put him in a LOUBOUTIN shoe bag and passed him around to you and your mom, and we all screamed at how cute he was.

What’s your favorite look that the three of us have ever done together, and why?

R: The VMAs, the white Alexandre Vauthier dress with the purple boots. Or the Tamara Ralph at the Oscars. We’ve had so many incredible moments, it’s hard to pick just one, but those are definitely up there.

M: I would agree with the white Alexander Vaulthier! It felt cool, effortless, and chic. But let’s add on to that with the 2017 Oscars Ralph and Russo. The “Afterlife” video had so many great looks. Dickinson Premiere! Too many press looks to pick, but I particularly love this Elie Saab moment. Also…THE WEDDING! Can’t forget those magical looks!

But if you could go back and change one, what would it be?

R: Honestly, no. I can’t think of one. Wait…can you?

M: NO NOTES.

What is your styling process? How does it work?

R: We always start by asking, “Who do we want to be?” That’s the fun part, especially with actresses who are also musicians, because you can push things a little further. We pull both together and separately, depending on the job, then bring everything into the office and set it up on the walls. Fittings are where it all comes together. Honestly, nine times out of ten, we end up going with the first thing we try on. You just feel it.

M: It all starts with the client and project or creative. We used to shop together in the earlier days, but as our business grew, we adapted to divide and conquer. As Rob mentioned, we have the same taste. Depending on the scale of the job, we always start with a game plan and then divvy up who goes where, who scours collections on Vogue.com. We always love to do fittings together when time permits, and if not, we have a shared photo album of all client fittings, and we discuss our “faves” after! Of course, ultimately it’s our client’s decision!

Unofficial stylist uniform? Anything you’re wearing and obsessed with?

R: Oversized and comfortable. Some kind of layer, a jacket, and usually a baseball hat. I really only wear a few colors: khaki, green, navy, and black. My closet kind of looks like a cartoon character. The same thing over and over.

M: I’m a big fan of less is more, unless we’re talking jewelry. As a working mom, I’m very into white t-shirts (Karla Welch’s specifically), vintage Levi’s, and a great loafer or slide. I don’t really have time to think too much about my looks since I’m raising two boys and have to think about my clients’ looks. My theory is that classics always win, plus layer up some jewelry to make it more of a personal aesthetic. It’s a good thing I married a jeweler…

How do you build a successful long-term relationship with someone you style or work with?

R: Trust, obviously. But also that we have fun. In a world that can be chaotic, we get the fun part. We’re playing with couture and diamonds. It’s an amazing job, and I think people feel that energy.

M: Trust first and foremost. I think creating a safe place to express, listen to, and share ideas. I think trust is built over time. Encouraging clients to share what they do and don’t like is important, but also being able to express when we feel strongly about trying something outside of their comfort zone. I think it’s important to allow for open ideas and not try to take control over anyone’s personal style. We are here to enhance and bring out the most beautiful version of ourselves.

Styling can be glamorous but also super chaotic. What’s the craziest scenario you’ve ever styled someone in?

R: We were trained in music, so honestly, we feel overly prepared most of the time. We have the best team, and we’re really buttoned up. We’ve had clients laugh that even our kits have kits. Zippers breaking happens more than you’d think, but we always have backups. We’re ready for anything.

M: I think I’m going to say JLo at the Super Bowl. The circumstances of a live performance, getting the stage and the entire cast ready to move to center field, and having multiple (six!) functional real time quick changes during the show — that’s probably the most high-stakes scenario. I think it felt chaotic because of the scale, but we were so buttoned up and rehearsed that we felt very, very prepared. Jen is incredibly professional and seasoned. She is such a pro, which also helps us in that high-stakes scenario!

What’s next for both of you? What’s exciting you right now in life and styling?

R: I absolutely love what I do. And recently, I started an outerwear project called AUTER, which has been really exciting creatively. You were actually one of the first people to wear it and support it, which meant a lot. Even Josh has a coat. I’m really proud of it, but styling is still very much my world.

M: What’s exciting me in life right now is raising my seven and eight-year-old sons! Whew! You have seen me through both pregnancies and held my firstborn! It really gives such a perspective on life and what matters most. I love being a stylist and working, AND I love being a mom. It is a lot of work, but I love both roles with a passion. I’ve always been cognizant of “what’s next” since the beginning of my career. I have a passion for so many things, from real estate (formerly an Airbnb super host), homes, interiors, creative directing, consulting, and working with private clients who love to collect couture clothing. I’m just open to what the universe brings next while I continue raising my sons and continuing to do what I love to do!

R&M’s QUICKFIRE BS:

Wake-up routine:

R: Coffee immediately.
M: 6:30 am, jump up and hit the ground running. Brush teeth, wash face, throw on a sweatsuit (Skims). Make breakfast, pack snacks, pack backpacks, get boys dressed, hair, teeth, in the car, and drop off. Then go home and reset for my day…and hit the ground running again. (Obviously, this is when we’re not on set.)

Jet lag hack:

R: Stay up as long as you can.
M: The second I get on the plane, I act like I’m in the time zone of my destination. So I go to sleep ASAP or stay up the whole flight, depending on where I’m going.

Red carpet survival snack:

R: McDonald’s drive-through.
M: I pack a bento box (mom life): hard-boiled egg, Parmesan cheese, and apples.

Advice you live by:

R: Trust your instinct.
M: Work hard, play hard, be kind.

Your favorite kind of positive BS:

R: Beauty Sleep. (Mariel’s better be Big Sunglasses.)
M: BLACK SUNGLASSES. ALL DAY EVERY DAY.

TO WATCH: A meeting of stylish minds. R&M styled me for this shoot. Rob was with me in New York, and we had the best time. (And yes, for those of you who asked, expect more interior design content!)

TO READ: Rob’s partner Nicolle’s Substack, Extremely Helpful. She reports on TV and movies and is hilarious.

TO FOLLOW: IZZI! I’m rarely on Instagram these days, but this girl is a great fashion follow. Case in point.

TO WEAR: A great pair of jeans, kitten-heeled sandals, and a deck of question cards to throw in your purse, all in this week’s “Leaving the house” edit.

TO KNOW ABOUT: The most popular handbag from the year you were born.

TO TAKE WITH YOU: “When we engage in what we are naturally suited to do, our work takes on the quality of play, and it is play that stimulates creativity.” -Linda Naiman

Love,
Hailee
(Beau)

“I was lucky enough to travel to Japan in the late seventies. Greer is spot on with her descriptions of what she experienced. A beautiful country with lovely people. Thanks for bringing back so many memories.” -Ann (MBS)

p_text:
I love fashion. I’ve always been intrigued and fascinated by it. Fashion touches nearly every aspect of my life: from the work I do as an actor, to events and appearances related to my work, to my ever-evolving personal style, which I’m especially excited to hone in this new stage of my life. My fashion curiosity has grown exponentially through my relationship with Rob Zangardi and Mariel Haenn, my two amazing stylists, who have taught me so much about style and brought me into their creative process in an incredibly collaborative way. The three of us have been friends for over a decade. R&M (that’s what I call them) have dressed me for music videos, MET Galas, Oscars, my wedding day, and every event in between. We’ll dive deeper into the stories of some of those specific fittings next week in MBS. But this week, I wanted R&M to come on to tell you about their careers because while they’ve taught me everything I know about fashion, they’ve also been instrumental in modeling how to work hard, trust yourself and others, form lasting relationships, and have a lot of fun along the way to building a dream life. In our conversation, we get into how these two first got their foot in the door of the fashion industry (TRL anyone?), what a day in the life of a stylist really looks like, plus we relive our favorite looks from the past ten years — some real throwbacks! Now onto my stylists and close friends: Rob and Mariel! R: I kind of fell into it. My twin brother Joey had moved to New York and was working in casting at MTV. He introduced me to the wardrobe department, and they took a chance on me. I was a kid from Ohio, suddenly in Times Square watching the ball drop on New Year’s Eve, surrounded by celebrities. It was one of those real “how did I get here?” moments. That was also the height of MTV and TRL. It was the most incredible time. A dream I could have never imagined, and honestly, the thing that set up the rest of my life. I owe everything to MTV and my brother. M: I was in high school and was always drawn to fashion. It was the ‘90s. Jennifer Lopez and Gwen Stefani videos were dominating the charts on the MTV Top Video Countdown. I would watch their videos and think, ‘I don’t know what it’s called, but I want to dress pop stars for their music videos!’ I was 15 years old. R: MTV back then had all the access in the world. Every celebrity came through. Red carpets, award shows, appearances, we had access to everything. It was also the very beginning of the Internet. We had actual Rolodexes filled with business cards. If we got a new VJ or client, like Mandy Moore, we would messenger over their comp cards to brands like Chanel to get them interested in dressing them. We were also working off physical lookbooks, not Vogue.com. You’d sit there flipping pages and making selects. Everything was somehow slower, but at the same time, we were still dealing with the same last-minute chaos we live in today. And if there was a big red carpet, you were hoping your work would end up in print a month later or in the newspaper the next day. The back of the tabloids was both our best friend and worst enemy. You could be best-dressed in Vogue and worst-dressed in Us Weekly for the exact same outfit. It was fun. It was chaotic. And it was everything. M: Working at MTV in the late ‘90s was such a vibe. We had so many different types of celebrities come through those hallways. In a way, it kinda felt like high school all over again. Standing in the hallways after getting my VJs dressed, waiting for the live guest of the day…it was exhilarating to say the least. It was also so much fun since I had already worked in the music industry prior to my job at MTV. It was like a reunion when I saw artists and managers I already had relationships with. It definitely felt like I was growing up and evolving from where I started. One of my greatest memories was waiting for the weeklies to come out (US Weekly, InStyle, etc.) to see how my work was judged, so to speak. I loved seeing any of the looks I styled printed in the magazines. R: We met at MTV a few years later and immediately became best friends. Not long after, we became partners. Our first job together was the Rihanna “Umbrella” video, which is kind of insane when you think about it. What an epic way to start a partnership. We came from completely different worlds, but somehow had the exact same eye. We would walk into a showroom and pull the exact same thing without even talking. It just always worked. I’m thankful for that partnership every day. M: Rob worked in the same wardrobe department as me, but he was there before I started. He came back to visit; he was friends with Suchin Pak (one of my VJs). We instantly connected. He said he needed to pull jewelry for a shoot he was doing. I said, “Why don’t you look at what I have…take whatever you need!” which in hindsight was very trusting, as we had just met! R: It was wild — green screen, rain, fire, Jay-Z, dancers, puddles everywhere. It definitely tested us. But it’s still one of the most memorable jobs we’ve ever done. M: Oh man…too many memories to count. All I can say is that there was a time when we only slept on airplanes. She was my first client and my first everything. I’ll always hold a special place in my heart for her! We grew up together and made such a statement with our work together. R: It feels like we’ve worked together forever. It feels like family…and I still can’t believe you’ve added to your family. Mariel and I are so excited for you and Josh, and we cannot wait to dress this baby. M: Oh gosh, I’ve literally been racking my brain. Honestly, I don’t remember the first time, but I have a very clear memory of when I rescued my dog Nigel and brought him to the office for a fitting. He was so small and cold that I put him in a LOUBOUTIN shoe bag and passed him around to you and your mom, and we all screamed at how cute he was. R: The VMAs, the white Alexandre Vauthier dress with the purple boots. Or the Tamara Ralph at the Oscars. We’ve had so many incredible moments, it’s hard to pick just one, but those are definitely up there. M: I would agree with the white Alexander Vaulthier! It felt cool, effortless, and chic. But let’s add on to that with the 2017 Oscars Ralph and Russo. The “Afterlife” video had so many great looks. Dickinson Premiere! Too many press looks to pick, but I particularly love this Elie Saab moment. Also…THE WEDDING! Can’t forget those magical looks! R: Honestly, no. I can’t think of one. Wait…can you? M: NO NOTES. R: We always start by asking, “Who do we want to be?” That’s the fun part, especially with actresses who are also musicians, because you can push things a little further. We pull both together and separately, depending on the job, then bring everything into the office and set it up on the walls. Fittings are where it all comes together. Honestly, nine times out of ten, we end up going with the first thing we try on. You just feel it. M: It all starts with the client and project or creative. We used to shop together in the earlier days, but as our business grew, we adapted to divide and conquer. As Rob mentioned, we have the same taste. Depending on the scale of the job, we always start with a game plan and then divvy up who goes where, who scours collections on Vogue.com. We always love to do fittings together when time permits, and if not, we have a shared photo album of all client fittings, and we discuss our “faves” after! Of course, ultimately it’s our client’s decision! R: Oversized and comfortable. Some kind of layer, a jacket, and usually a baseball hat. I really only wear a few colors: khaki, green, navy, and black. My closet kind of looks like a cartoon character. The same thing over and over. M: I’m a big fan of less is more, unless we’re talking jewelry. As a working mom, I’m very into white t-shirts (Karla Welch’s specifically), vintage Levi’s, and a great loafer or slide. I don’t really have time to think too much about my looks since I’m raising two boys and have to think about my clients’ looks. My theory is that classics always win, plus layer up some jewelry to make it more of a personal aesthetic. It’s a good thing I married a jeweler… R: Trust, obviously. But also that we have fun. In a world that can be chaotic, we get the fun part. We’re playing with couture and diamonds. It’s an amazing job, and I think people feel that energy.

M: Trust first and foremost. I think creating a safe place to express, listen to, and share ideas. I think trust is built over time. Encouraging clients to share what they do and don’t like is important, but also being able to express when we feel strongly about trying something outside of their comfort zone. I think it’s important to allow for open ideas and not try to take control over anyone’s personal style. We are here to enhance and bring out the most beautiful version of ourselves. R: We were trained in music, so honestly, we feel overly prepared most of the time. We have the best team, and we’re really buttoned up. We’ve had clients laugh that even our kits have kits. Zippers breaking happens more than you’d think, but we always have backups. We’re ready for anything. M: I think I’m going to say JLo at the Super Bowl. The circumstances of a live performance, getting the stage and the entire cast ready to move to center field, and having multiple (six!) functional real time quick changes during the show — that’s probably the most high-stakes scenario. I think it felt chaotic because of the scale, but we were so buttoned up and rehearsed that we felt very, very prepared. Jen is incredibly professional and seasoned. She is such a pro, which also helps us in that high-stakes scenario! R: I absolutely love what I do. And recently, I started an outerwear project called AUTER, which has been really exciting creatively. You were actually one of the first people to wear it and support it, which meant a lot. Even Josh has a coat. I’m really proud of it, but styling is still very much my world. M: What’s exciting me in life right now is raising my seven and eight-year-old sons! Whew! You have seen me through both pregnancies and held my firstborn! It really gives such a perspective on life and what matters most. I love being a stylist and working, AND I love being a mom. It is a lot of work, but I love both roles with a passion. I’ve always been cognizant of “what’s next” since the beginning of my career. I have a passion for so many things, from real estate (formerly an Airbnb super host), homes, interiors, creative directing, consulting, and working with private clients who love to collect couture clothing. I’m just open to what the universe brings next while I continue raising my sons and continuing to do what I love to do! R: Coffee immediately.
M: 6:30 am, jump up and hit the ground running. Brush teeth, wash face, throw on a sweatsuit (Skims). Make breakfast, pack snacks, pack backpacks, get boys dressed, hair, teeth, in the car, and drop off. Then go home and reset for my day…and hit the ground running again. (Obviously, this is when we’re not on set.) R: Stay up as long as you can.
M: The second I get on the plane, I act like I’m in the time zone of my destination. So I go to sleep ASAP or stay up the whole flight, depending on where I’m going. R: McDonald’s drive-through.
M: I pack a bento box (mom life): hard-boiled egg, Parmesan cheese, and apples. R: Trust your instinct.
M: Work hard, play hard, be kind. R: Beauty Sleep. (Mariel’s better be Big Sunglasses.)
M: BLACK SUNGLASSES. ALL DAY EVERY DAY. TO WATCH: A meeting of stylish minds. R&M styled me for this shoot. Rob was with me in New York, and we had the best time. (And yes, for those of you who asked, expect more interior design content!) TO READ: Rob’s partner Nicolle’s Substack, Extremely Helpful. She reports on TV and movies and is hilarious. TO FOLLOW: IZZI! I’m rarely on Instagram these days, but this girl is a great fashion follow. Case in point. TO WEAR: A great pair of jeans, kitten-heeled sandals, and a deck of question cards to throw in your purse, all in this week’s “Leaving the house” edit. TO KNOW ABOUT: The most popular handbag from the year you were born. TO TAKE WITH YOU: “When we engage in what we are naturally suited to do, our work takes on the quality of play, and it is play that stimulates creativity.” -Linda Naiman Love,Hailee(Beau) Highlighting your comments on the previous week’s issue. Today, Ann’s response to Meet my closest friends. “I was lucky enough to travel to Japan in the late seventies. Greer is spot on with her descriptions of what she experienced. A beautiful country with lovely people. Thanks for bringing back so many memories.” -Ann (MBS) Subscribe for all the BS

title: Marching into spring — BEAU SOCIETY issue #078
author: beausociety
raw_article:
Hi, my loves! Happy spring! Happy Aries season! I don’t usually follow astrology…but it looks like we could be in for some action this month. Are you contemplating a haircut? I’m a big fan of a springtime chop. How about spring cleaning? I just did a gigantic closet cleanout to get rid of clothes I haven’t worn in years, and I feel a million pounds lighter. In the vein of detoxing and making space, I thought it would be a great time to clean out my camera roll. You all loved February’s fun little recap letter, so here’s the March version!

This month, we celebrated my mom’s birthday, which always makes me pause and reflect on how grateful I am for her. In between folding baby clothes and trying to kick a killer cold, I remembered an Emily Dickinson poem I love, savored the extra hour of sunlight post daylight savings, and cried tears of joy at the television. More on all of it, ahead. Plus, what our readers are focused on this spring and a Spring Refresh Edit I’m particularly obsessed with.

Haircuts!

Earlier this month, Teenie and Brando got big chops. Ready for spring!

Melon sorbet & whipped cream

My favorite sweet of the month (besides the cookies) was this melon sorbet with a ton of whipped cream. Can’t recommend this combo enough. It was a heatwave in LA, and this hit the spot. Rainbow sprinkles are always an easy add.

A “stop and smell the flowers” moment

I’ve had a lot of these this month, little signs from the universe to take a breath and feel my feet on the ground. Just the other day, I was on a walk, and this bee was buzzing right in front of my face. Naturally, I froze (is that a natural instinct??), hoping that if I continued to mind my business, they would mind theirs. Then I followed its flight path straight into this flower and watched it harvest pollen. I don’t think I’ve ever seen that before, have you? Maybe I have, and I just wasn’t present enough, or I was more concerned about getting away from the bee. It stopped me in the best way. As I continued my walk, I found myself searching for the words from this poem I love:

Thank God for moms, am I right?

Feeling extra grateful for my Momma this month as we celebrate her most recent trip around the sun. Her love and support have always been a steady rhythm in my life, constant and unwavering. She has set a standard that I don’t know if I’ll ever be able to match, but I’m sure going to try.

Lighter later

It’s always so nice to feel like there’s a little more time in the day, isn’t it? That’s how I’ve been feeling since we turned the clocks. A sunset pic never does the real thing justice, but here’s my best attempt with one we caught last week.

I’ve always had a thing for scrapbooking…

I’ve recently gotten back into scrapbooking at the same time as one of my best friends. We’re doing it together, which has been so fun. Josh makes fun of how often we’re voicenoting about our scrapbooks. We call our casual little sessions ‘scrap and yap,’ and I always look forward to them! Plus, I’ve found that a scrapbook is not only the perfect place for all my polaroids, but it houses all the random things I want to hold onto but don’t have a place for. Now my sonograms aren’t just getting rolled up and stored away - they have a special page!

And knitting!

I recently became reacquainted with my knitting needles, and now I can’t put them down! There’s something so satisfying about knitting a row and seeing the neat progression. My friends tell me they love knitting and needlepoint because it keeps them off their phones, which is a big fringe benefit for me, too.

Crying over the Oscars

Couldn’t write a reflection on this month without mentioning Sinners, specifically the love I felt watching my cast on Oscars night. Despite being at home, I was overwhelmed with pride and joy for this cast. It’s been so cool to see so many of you rewatching Sinners and going back to old press interviews from the premiere. (Also remember our Beau Society cast Q&A from way back when!?). It’s been such an unexpected joy to get to live in this experience for a little bit longer.

Earlier this week, I asked our More BS readers what they’re up to this spring…

“New Year’s resolutions are one thing, but Spring resolutions are where it’s at! I started sitting down at my piano again. It’s like riding a bike. I never forgot, but the training wheels did need to go back on. Slowly but surely, it’s all coming back to me.” -Maria

“I’m stepping into spring by tackling a backlog of books I built up. Just hoping for warmer weather so I can read them outside in the sun!” -Nick

“Planning my 30th birthday party for next week! preparing for a brand new chapter (and decade).” -Emily

“I’m stepping into spring by starting a garden!! I ordered some vegetable seeds the other day, and I can’t wait for them to come in the mail.” -Hannah

“I dyed my hair😇 I needed a little change for the spring and summer.” -Radka

“Set up a sewing room, starting to make summery dresses!!” -Rena

“Spending spring break with myself and learning to love it!” -Emerson

Some great ideas! Before you go, shop our Spring Refresh Edit. It’s one of my favorites!! So many gems, like the most gorgeous (faux) floral arrangement you’ve ever seen, soft white pants, and this journal I found at a really cute shop in LA. These plus 24 more picks in this week’s edit.

SPRING REFRESH EDIT

TO READ: Broken Country by Clare Leslie Hall. A few friends have been raving about this one.

TO WEAR: This powder pink bandana from the new Agolde x Maria McManus collection. I love everything.

TO WATCH: Maggie and Jake Gyllenhaal making banana bread.

TO TAKE WITH YOU: “When you need clarity, subtract.” -James Clear

Okay, that’s all for now! I have a special extra issue planned for MBS readers dropping early next week. It’s all about the journey of building Beau Society and connecting with all of you. Then next Friday, we have a travel diary you won’t want to miss — a trip to Japan is in your future!

Love,
Hailee
(Beau)

Highlighting your comments on the previous week’s issue. Today, Ilke’s response to Cookies and milk (preferably in a martini glass).

“Since it’s Eid right now, my mom’s been making all those classic Turkish cookies and cakes. She’s got this notebook filled with recipes from my grandma, her friends, and neighbors — she basically hand-copied everything in there. It’s got everything from traditional Turkish sweets to desserts from all over the world. To me, that book is like a window into my mom’s past, and it’s just packed with memories. I showed her this issue, and she’s obsessed with the cookie recipe! It’s definitely getting a spot in her recipe book.❤️❤️ -Ilke (MBS)

Subscribe for all the BS

title: Meet my closest friends — BEAU SOCIETY issue #079
author: beausociety
raw_article:
Hi, my loves!! Happy Friday! Thank you for all your kind words and messages yesterday! To say we are unbelievably happy and in love with this baby girl would be an understatement.

I’m soaking in the newborn bliss, but I’ll still be writing here every week. I actually couldn’t be more excited for these next few Friday issues, which I’m co-writing with my closest friends!!! Since I started Beau Society, I’ve wanted to feature the people in my innermost circle and share their stories and perspectives with all of you. I’m obviously biased, but they’re so much fun, and I know you’ll love them! It makes sense to kick it off with a letter I wrote with someone you already know and loved so much the first time…

GREER GUSTAVSON!

If you’ve been reading Beau Society for a while, you know that Greer is one of my best friends and has been by my side for nearly every important moment over the past five years. We were living together when I started dreaming up the idea for Beau Society! We’re both born and raised in LA and share weirdly similar tastes in books (and newsletters), clothes, music, and the topic of today’s letter: travel!!

Earlier this year, Greer took this amazing, spontaneous trip to Japan with her husband and a couple of friends. When she came back and told me about it and showed me the photos(!), I begged her to write a little travel diary for Beau Society. Luckily, she was down! Plus, she pulled in Jess Bazan, a professional travel advisor and a friend of ours, to give us the inside scoop on top travel destinations for 2026. (How gorgeous is Hotel Yellowstone??) Plus, Jess gives us her expert carry-on tips, like the throat spray she always keeps in her bag to avoid getting sick and the best cure for jet lag I’ve ever heard.

Handing it over to Greer to take us through her incredible trip to Japan…

Hi everyone, Greer here!

I recently got married, and after our wedding, Demitri (my husband) and I told each other that, while we’re young, we’d try to be yes people and take advantage of any opportunity to go on an adventure together. I’m a planner by nature, so spur-of-the-moment trips are not usually in my wheelhouse. But when we realized we had two free weeks in February (rare for us these days), we started dreaming up a trip to Japan, which has been at the top of our ‘dream destinations’ list since we started dating.

I channeled that yes-person energy and got to thinking about our itinerary, which, for me, usually begins with calling Jess, one of our best friends and our built-in travel agent extraordinaire (more tips from her later). As it happened, Jess already had a work trip to Japan scheduled for the same week we were thinking about…so a slightly impromptu group trip seemed like fate! Japan has become the go-to spot for honeymoons, and for good reason, but if you ever have a chance to go with friends, I absolutely recommend it! We had so much fun exploring (and eating) as a group and soaking up everything Japan has to offer!

A little bit about the specifics of our trip:

Set a manageable itinerary:

We had nine days total, so we hit two different cities: Tokyo and Kyoto. We spent five days in Tokyo, then took the bullet train (highly recommend!!) to Kyoto and spent four days there.

Make a short priority list:

I went into this trip without much of a plan at all — Hailee, you know how tough this was for me! — but I loved being detached from an itinerary. When you’re overly concerned about hitting everything on a to-do list, it’s easy to miss the magic a city or country has to offer. Instead, I ended up making a short priority list of things I wanted to do while I was in Japan. One was as vague as “try a random food at 7/11,” and another was a specific temple I’d heard about that I wanted to visit. I fully untied myself from the idea that I’d hit every “hot spot.”

Skip the Instagram recs:

This trip was a good reminder not to get all your travel recommendations on Instagram! Leading up to our trip, my feed was made up entirely of “what to do in Japan” videos. While these can be very fun and useful jumping-off points, I am so glad I didn’t limit our experience to just the “trendy” spots. You can walk down any street in Japan and find amazing food and hidden treasures to explore. Not to mention that the places we found on Instagram all had lines. I hate to say that waiting an hour for a matcha isn’t worth it, but it probably isn’t.

Unexpected highlights in Japan:

Japan is one of the most amazing places we’ve ever visited from a cultural perspective. The Japanese people are so intentional in the way they do things. Everyone was so kind and hospitable. And there are so many unique cultural norms that you pick up on right away. Some that really stood out to me:

  • It’s the cleanest place I’ve been in my life. The streets are spotless. There are no trash cans anywhere. It’s not a to-go city. You’re encouraged to eat and drink at the place where you bought your food and use the trash can there. A good reminder to slow our pace a bit.

  • It is so quiet. The streets and the public transportation are almost silent compared to LA or NY. There’s also a “women only” section of the train, which I thought was so amazing, even though I didn’t get to use it on this trip without ditching Demitri.

  • Simple technological advancements can really change your day. In our hotel elevator, you could unclick a floor that you pressed accidentally. Hello? Why do we not have that capability in elevators here? They really are 10 steps ahead.

  • Everything was so thoughtful. I noticed this a lot in stores and restaurants, though I am sure there are a million ways this manifests throughout Japan. The vintage clothing is so well cared for, restaurants focus on perfecting one particular item instead of a full range, and snacks are efficiently wrapped for easy opening. The Japanese are specific in a really cool way, and it shows.

BEST BITE: Hikiniku To Come

This restaurant popped up many times on my Instagram feed before our trip, and it was one of the few that was totally worth it. Since Kyoto is close to the city of Kobe, you naturally run into a lot of wagyu on menus. At this particular place, they make one single type of beef patty, which they serve with unlimited white rice and different toppings and dipping sauces. It seems worth mentioning that the rice refills were unlimited — mind you, this was the best white rice I’ve ever had in my life!

BEST SHOPPING: Vintage!

There’s a ton of great vintage in Japan. The collection of vintage designer handbags in Tokyo is a sight to see in and of itself. I came home with a special clutch from the ‘90s, and Demitri absolutely scored in the clothing department. Japanese streetwear. Japanese denim. We described almost everything as “effortlessly cool.”

BEST 7/11 PICK: Egg salad sandwich

If you know, you know. I was ~hesitant~ but happily surprised to find that the online hype wasn’t undeserved here.

I love insider travel advice, so when Greer mentioned she was traveling to Japan with Jess Bazan, her dear friend who was just named a top travel agent of 2026 by Condé Nast Traveler(!), I texted her a quick BS travel Q&A….

Let’s start with packing. What’s in your carry-on?

First things first, the best carry-on on the market is the Calpak Luka Duffel. I am always amazed by how much I can manage to squeeze in there. My carry-on must-haves are:

  • Tower 27 SOS Spray

  • Noshinku Hand Sanitizer

  • Laniege Lip Mask

  • Cymbiotika Magnesium

  • Barefoot Dreams sleep mask and fuzzy socks

  • Nighty Night tea

  • Kindle

  • AirPods

  • An empty pouch - I collect hotel stationery and paper goods from trips, so I also bring a pouch for my findings!

What’s the one place you think everyone should visit at least once in their lives?

Keeping in theme – Tokyo, Japan!

How do you find your travel inspiration and unique destinations to recommend to your clients?

As a travel advisor, I sit in an interesting position: I’m constantly gathering inspiration not just for my own taste, but for my clients’ too. Recommendations are incredibly personal, so a big part of my job is understanding the tastemakers in my clients’ lives and the aesthetic they’re drawn to, then matching them to the right places.

Personally, Substack is my happy place for inspiration. A few newsletters I always trust to send me somewhere great are Happy Hoteling, Strictly the Good Stuff, and Atelier Le Monde. I also love larger publications like Italy Segreta, Yolo Journal, Net-a-Porter, and Robb Report.

A rule of thumb: find people whose taste you love, whether they’re interior designers, fashion people, or writers, and pay attention to where they travel. Chances are, if you love their style, you’ll probably love their hotel taste too!

Top 5 travel destinations on your radar for 2026, go:

  1. Greece - One of my favorite summer travel destinations. There is so much new coming, like the Rosewood Blue Palace in Crete. Clients are looking into lesser-touristed islands like Folegandros, where I am itching to stay at Gundari.

  2. Harbour Island, Bahamas - An island where you are forced to slow down and golf cart around, with perfectly pink-hued sand. A weekend at Coral Sands sounds lovely.

  3. Egypt - Particularly, sailing through the Nile on a luxury vessel like the Nour el Nil.

  4. South Africa - I am planning a South African honeymoon and want to end at a beach resort. Benguerra Island in Mozambique has been particularly on the mind at Kisawa Sanctuary or &Beyond Benguerra Island.

  5. Jackson Hole, Wyoming - I’ve never been and would love to go in the summertime to Hotel Yellowstone.

Most underrated travel destination?

Canada! Stunning nature from Whistler to Lake Louise. Great cities like Vancouver and Montreal. Truly special luxury properties like Nimmo Bay Retreat or Fogo Island Inn.

Most overrated travel destination?

The answer to this question is totally based on personal preference, so without calling anyone’s baby ugly, I plead the fifth…

Best suitcase:

Beis for roller luggage. Calpak for duffels and packing cubes.

Best cure for jetlag?

An IV drip, no napping, and a double shot of espresso. Give yourself grace on the return and an extra day off work to fully reset, unpack, and reorient, if you can.

Best immune supplement?

Erewhon Liposomal Vitamin C and Beekeeper’s Naturals Throat Spray.

Carry-on or check a bag?

My rule is carry-on always, unless it is over two weeks or it’s a cold-weather trip!

When traveling, always overplan or underplan?

A bit of both. Personally, I like to plan 2-3 prescheduled activities through the trip, but leave a lot up to wandering and spontaneity. I come prepared with a general outline of neighborhoods I’d like to visit — its stores, sights, cocktail bars, etc. — that I can turn to if I’ve wandered too aimlessly and need direction.

What’s the hotel you’d live in:

San Ysidro Ranch.

And your room service order?

Caesar salad and fries.

TO PACK: A great travel pouch. For your findings.

TO KNOW: What actually gets confiscated by TSA.

TO WATCH: “I got a thing for hotel stationery.”

TO SHOP: Our BS travel edit, informed by Jess’s amazing recs and our team’s favorite travel must-haves.

TO TAKE WITH YOU: “Attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity.” – Simone Weil

Hope you have some spring break travel coming up — or maybe this inspires you to book a trip to Japan for summer or fall! Let me know where you’re going. Next week, I’m sharing my favorite travel memories from being on tour, and we have a very special fashion issue with my stylists!! Sign up for More BS so you get all of it. Happy to have you here, and as always, thanks for reading!!

Subscribe now

Love,
Hailee
(Beau)

Highlighting your comments on the previous week’s issue. Today, Bria’s response to Marching into spring.

“I love how you mentioned the ‘stop and smell the flowers’ moment/quote. Just the other day, I saw a clip from The Drew Barrymore Show that made me look at that quote, flowers, and the beauty of spring differently this time around. Someone asked how to make flowers last longer, and this florist’s response blew me away. He said, ‘Flowers are not supposed to last, their job is to help us stay present.’ I’m so beyond happy to hear you’ve taken this time to really remain present in your life, and I hope our BS community does too. <3. Happy Spring, everyone.” -Bria (MBS)

title: Cookies and milk (preferably in a martini glass) — BEAU SOCIETY issue #077
author: beausociety
raw_article:
I love a big, hardcover cookbook marked with Post-It Notes and dog-eared pages. There’s something so satisfying about pulling a cookbook off a shelf and reading through a great recipe that’s tangible in your hands and not on your iPad. I prefer it!

A few weeks ago, I was gifted an early copy of Cookie Club, a gorgeous cookbook devoted entirely to cookies. Turning the pages, I immediately fell in love with the cozy writing, helpful cookie-baking tips (cookie spread too much? Not enough flour!), creative hosting ideas, and little paintings sprinkled throughout. Of course, I wanted to bake every cookie (there are 80!) immediately.

Cookie Club author Mallory Oiniki devotes her entire first chapter to the art of the perfect chocolate chip cookie — thick and gooey, thin and crispy, and the Goldilocks: chewy with pools of melted chocolate (my personal favorite - that recipe, ahead!). The second chapter covers the classics, like M&M, oatmeal raisin, and double chocolate. Then Cookie Club diverts delightfully left-of-center, and you get cookies like miso toffee snickerdoodle, crème brûlée (yep, flagged that immediately!), and banana pudding, which is basically Magnolia Banana Pudding in cookie form. Mallory fires on all cylinders. There’s a chapter in the book titled ‘Cookies Inspired by TV Shows and Movies’ that feels very BS-aligned. Every time I flip through Cookie Club, I find another thing to love. Last week, I jumped on a Zoom with Mallory to dig into how she built a career around cookies (my dream?), her creative inspiration, plus, she drops her go-to baking playlist and the best chocolate chip cookie recipe from her new cookbook. Beau Appétit!

I’m so inspired by people like you who take something they love, like baking cookies, and make a full career out of it. How did you find your way to writing a cookie cookbook?

I’ve always loved cookies. Everyone loves cookies! They’re such a shareable treat. Some baked goods are just too scary for people for good reason, if they’re really complicated or technical. But cookies...everyone can at least try to make a cookie. They’re so approachable, and that’s something I really gravitated towards.

In 2020, when we were all at home making banana bread and sourdough, I started posting my recipes online, but never with the intention of turning it into a career. I’ve always loved cooking and baking, so it started as just a fun hobby. Then I started the series that I called ‘Cookies Inspired by TV Shows and Movies.’ I did eight or nine cookies in that series, and they all popped off and went super viral. That was so fun! I would take any element of the show or movie I was watching and try to find a cookie idea that fit naturally with it, but that was creative and out of the box. I did that series for about six months, briefly stopped it, and brought it back in the book, which I’m so excited about. There are a bunch of new ones!

So I say I kind of stumbled into my dream job. I definitely think it was a lot of luck and good timing, as I think a lot of things in life are. I could not be more grateful. I always wanted to write a cookbook, but I just thought that wouldn’t happen for a long time, so when the opportunity presented itself, I was over the moon. And obviously, cookies were what I was most excited about. I post savory foods as well and share those recipes, but cookies are definitely what I’m best known for, so that made the most sense to turn into a cookbook, and I’m so glad I did it.

Your ‘Cookies Inspired by TV Shows and Movies’ kind of put you on the map! What’s the cookie inspired by a TV or movie that went most viral?

The first one I made was a Stranger Things cookie because that show was big at the time. Then I just started asking people in the comments about what TV show or movie I should do next. Gilmore Girls was always at the top of the list. I had never seen Gilmore Girls. I grew up with three older brothers, so I didn’t have a lot of control over the TV. But once I started working on this series, I dove in and binged it, and was like, obviously this cookie has to be something around coffee, and so a Gilmore Girls Coffee Cake Cookie was perfect. That’s probably still the most popular cookie on my website. I’ve gotten so many DMs from people being like, “I made these and watched the show with my mom, and it was such a sweet experience.” It’s so fun to see how people use the cookies to connect with someone, and then the show or movie also connects them.

What’s the cookie inspired by a movie or TV show that you could eat every day?

Taste-wise, one of my favorites is the Ted Lasso Shortbread Cookies. They’re everything you want in shortbread: buttery, flaky, with turbinado sugar on top so you get that crunch. I could eat so many of those. And they’re not too sweet. It’s kind of ironic that I wrote a cookie cookbook because I don’t like overly sweet things. My cookies have a decent amount of salt because I like a balanced cookie, not an overly sweet cookie that you can only take one bite of. But shortbread especially is that way because it’s so butter-focused. Those are really fun ones. They’re so cute when you put them in a little box like in Ted Lasso.

But I mean also, and truly, this is gonna sound like I’m sucking up, but Sinners is incredible, and it would be so fun to make a cookie inspired by Sinners.

I’d love to try that cookie! You also write about the art of gathering around cookies, which I love. Talk to me about how to throw a cookie party.

We so often associate cookie parties with the holidays, which is great, but there are so many cookie flavors that aren’t holiday-specific that are perfect for hosting. One of the parties I love that I talk about in the book is a ‘Girls Night In.’ In that chapter, there’s a picture of me with a tub of my edible cookie dough, which is a really popular recipe and everything I want in a girls’ night in. In that chapter, I also include my Banana Pudding Cookies, which are really fun and cozy. If you like Magnolia Banana Pudding, it’s essentially that in cookie form. It’s a vanilla wafer cookie that’s really soft, rolled in vanilla wafer crumbs, topped with a pudding frosting, and fresh bananas on top. I think, and I write about this in the book, that cookies are the perfect treat to connect with people and find that common ground we need a lot more of nowadays.

You also have a section in the book about ‘Cookies For Your Star Sign’ — so fun! What was that process like, creating cookies based on astrology?

It was so fun to write that section! I know a decent amount about star signs, but I really had to dive deep to write that section and figure out the intricacies. That would be a really fun cookie party as well! Have people come over and bring the cookie of their star sign. People are so proud of their sign and have strong ownership over it.

What’s your star sign cookie?

I’m a Virgo, and it’s an Almond Croissant Cookie — I’m not just saying this, it’s really good. Virgos love perfection and orderliness, and the Almond Croissant Cookie essentially tastes like an almond croissant; it has frangipane in the dough and slivered almonds, and it’s dusted with powdered sugar. It’s a very pristine and elegant cookie. If you love almond croissants, it’s for you. What sign are you?

I’m a Sagittarius!

Sagittarius is a Blood Orange Cardamom and Pistachio Cookie. They’re adventurous, always chasing the next thrill. They’re daring, bright, and always down to try something new!

I love the illustrations in this book. Are they paintings?

Most people use illustrations in cookbooks, but I wanted paintings. I have a friend from high school who’s an incredible artist, her name is Giovana Cavalcante, and her artwork handle is ‘I make art not sense.’ I’m obsessed with her stuff, so I just went to her and was like, hey, would you like to do some paintings for the book? She said yes, and she hand-painted all of these paintings, and we scanned them in. She did a really amazing job. She really captured the vibe I was going for. The painting in the Chocolate Chip Cookie chapter is of my daughter, who just turned four, reaching for a chocolate chip cookie. With a cookie cookbook, I hope it’s something people are baking from and using, but I also wanted it to be a beautiful book people want to leave out. I think the paintings really help with that.

I love that! Does your daughter have a favorite cookie?

She would eat anything. She loves the edible cookie dough and always wants to lick the bowl! The cookbook process takes a long time. I’ve been working on this for two years — 80 cookies is a lot! I had to test each cookie five-plus times to really get it right. So she tested a lot of the cookies with me. At four, she knows how to crack an egg and turn on and off the stand mixer!

Most underrated kitchen tool: A baking scale makes a huge difference! You can get one for $30. I grew up using measuring cups, which work great too, but a kitchen scale makes your recipes a lot more accurate. If you want to level up your baking, that’s a really easy way to do it.

And overrated: I’m not a big apron person. I just risk it. Unless I’m searing meat and I don’t want oil splattered on me, I just go for it. I can dust off the flour.

Best baking playlist: Olivia Dean. It’s been Olivia Dean for years. I’m so excited for her growth and explosion. I love romanticizing cooking and baking, and turning on some music you love is a really great way to do that.

A recipe you saved or screenshotted recently: This brothy bean recipe with miso and green onions. I’m dying to try it. You slow-cook beans for a couple of hours with miso paste. It’s savory and umami, and finished off with some scallions. I’ll be making that one soon.

The secret to amazing food pics: I take all my pictures for my website and my normal content, but for a cookbook, you hire a full team. We had a photographer, a food stylist, and a food assistant. It was a whole operation. We made and photographed 80 cookies in 10 business days. It’s a lot. We used all the little tricks of food styling, like using makeup sponges to hold up cookies and make the perfect stack. [This makes me feel better about my own food photos!]

A cookbook everyone should own: I love Molly Baz’s Cook This Book.

Your “trust me, just try it!” cookie: The Chili Crisp Cookie is probably my most insane cookie. It’s really good — you really have to try it. I put the chili crisp in a brittle, so it’s contained because I didn’t want the whole cookie to be spicy and overwhelming. But you have these tiny bites of spice, then it’s paired with sesame seeds and coconut. That’s a really fun and pushes what people expect from a cookie.

Baking advice that applies to life: Don’t take it too seriously. We’re all floating on a spinning rock. If you want to go for something in life, go for it. If you’re embarrassed, don’t be. No one really cares at the end of the day. Same with baking. People don’t try things because they think, oh, I can’t do that. But it just takes trying and putting yourself out there in any part of life, baking included.

I feel so inspired by Mallory. If you love something, it can never be the wrong path. Go for it and don’t hold back. It reminds me of this letter. The one thing I was looking for was connection — actual, genuine connection, not the kind you get on social media, but a way to find a common ground with somebody. I hope you get a hard copy of Cookie Club (out April 14th!) and make cookies with or for someone you love! I plan to!

Mallory was kind enough to give us the full recipe for my favorite chocolate chip cookie from her book: the Brown Butter Chocolate Chip Cookies (BB CCCs). I can’t tell you how easy these are — if you can brown butter, you’re halfway there. I made them the other night when I was craving something sweet, and wow: chewy, chocolatey, and balanced from the flaky salt! I didn’t chill the dough for 12 hours (couldn’t wait!), but I will definitely be doing that next time.

Make these ASAP and let me know how it goes. Also, I’m curious, what’s your baking playlist??

Mallory’s BB CCCs (Brown Butter Chocolate Chip Cookies)

Dare I say there is nothing better than a brown butter chocolate chip cookie? I still remember the day I used brown butter in a chocolate chip cookie for the first time, and my life has never been the same. There is something about nutty brown butter + brown sugar + melty pools of chocolate that does it for me every time. These BB CCCs (as I affectionately refer to them) are crispy on the outside, chewy on the inside, and topped with plenty of flaky salt. The overnight rest is crucial for the texture, but waiting is definitely the hardest part! -Mallory

Makes 8 Large Cookies

Ingredients

½ cup (113 g) salted butter, cubed
1 small ice cube
½ cup (108 g) brown sugar
¼ cup (50 g) granulated sugar
1 large egg, room temperature
1 teaspoon vanilla extract
1 ¼ cups (162 g) all-purpose flour
½ tablespoon cornstarch
½ teaspoon baking soda
¾ teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt
¾ cup (128 g) chocolate chunks (milk, semisweet, dark, or a mix), plus extra for topping (see Note 1)
Flaky salt, for topping

Directions

  1. Heat a large pan over medium heat. Add the butter and stir until melted, about 1 minute. Keep stirring as the melted butter starts to bubble and then foam, about 2 minutes. After the foam subsides, you’ll see brown flecks on the bottom of the pan, and the butter will smell very nutty. Pour the brown butter into a small heat-safe bowl, add the ice cube, and stir until the ice cube melts. Let the butter cool to room temperature. (If you are in a hurry, cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a lid and transfer to the fridge or freezer to speed up the process

  2. Transfer the butter to a large bowl with the brown sugar and granulated sugar. Using a stand mixer or hand mixer, mix until fluffy and lighter in color, about 2 minutes.

  3. Add the egg and vanilla extract. Mix until combined, about 1 minute.

  4. Add the flour, cornstarch, baking soda, and kosher salt. Mix until just combined, about 30 seconds.

  5. Add the chocolate chunks and mix until evenly distributed through the dough.

  6. Line a baking sheet with parchment paper. Use a large (4-tablespoon/2 ounce) cookie scoop to scoop the dough into balls. Top each dough ball with one or two chunks of chocolate, gently pressing the chunks into the dough.

  7. Cover the pan with plastic wrap and then place in the fridge and let the dough chill for at least 12 hours or up to 72 hours (see Note 2).

  8. To bake the cookies (see Note 3), preheat the oven to 350 F (180 C). Line two baking sheets with parchment paper.

  9. Transfer up to four dough balls onto each baking sheet, spaced equally apart. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes, until the edges of the cookies are just starting to brown. Let the cookies cool on the baking sheets for 10 minutes and then transfer to a cooling rack to cool completely (or enjoy hot for an ooey-gooey cookie!). Top each cookie with a pinch of flaky salt.

Notes:

  1. Feel free to use chocolate chips instead of chunks! You won’t have the same pools of melted chocolate in and on top of the cookie, but chips will get the job done.

  2. The overnight rest is crucial for these cookies. It allows the brown butter flavor to fully seep into the dough and prevents the cookies from spreading too much.

  3. After the overnight rest, bake these cookies straight from the fridge! Don’t let the dough come up to room temperature, or the cookies will spread too much.

TO TRY: Milk in a vintage martini glass!

TO STOCK: Chili crunch! If you add this to your cookies, I’m impressed.

TO TREASURE: A whimsical little dish to house your soon-to-be-brown butter.

TO CLEAN WITH: My favorite kitchen counter cleaner. About time for a restock.

TO TAKE WITH YOU: “Baking is about precision, but it’s also about learning to trust yourself.” -Claire Saffitz

Love,
Hailee
(Beau)
